data.title: Is Broadcom Stock A Buy Now? Analysts See AI Demand Powering AVGO Higher
data.description:
Broadcom Inc (NASDAQ:AVGO) shares are down on Friday as the company is drawing growing analyst support amid a mixed market backdrop. Here’s what investors need to know.

  • Broadcom stock is facing resistance. What’s driving AVGO stock lower?

Are Hyperscalers Creating A Pricing Boom?

Analysts from TD Cowen have reaffirmed a Buy rating on Broadcom, setting a price target of $450, citing rising demand for its chips as artificial intelligence adoption accelerates. Additionally, KeyBanc’s analyst noted that hyperscalers are locking in DRAM and NAND capacity, which is expected to drive significant increases in contract pricing.

The analysts also highlighted Broadcom’s potential benefits from custom silicon, particularly with its Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate supply outlook for 2026. This comes as hyperscaler demand tightens memory supply, suggesting a robust long-term opportunity for Broadcom as it expands its AI backlog.

How Broadcom Makes Money – And Why AI Is Central To Its Future

Broadcom operates a diversified chip and infrastructure software business, with most of its revenue coming from high-performance semiconductors used in data centers, networking gear, storage, smartphones and broadband equipment.

The company also sells software used by enterprises to manage security, mainframes and complex IT infrastructure, providing a steady stream of recurring revenue. Artificial intelligence is becoming a key growth engine for Broadcom because AI workloads demand massive data movement and low-latency connections inside cloud data centers.

That plays directly to Broadcom's strengths in custom accelerators and high-speed networking chips, which help hyperscale customers build and scale AI clusters more efficiently.

Bearish Indicators Suggest Caution Ahead

The stock is currently trading 6.8% below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 10.2% below its 50-day SMA, indicating a bearish trend in the short term. Over the past 12 months, shares have increased by 33.42% and are currently positioned closer to their 52-week highs than lows, reflecting a strong longer-term performance.

The RSI is at 38.34, which is considered neutral territory, while the MACD is below its signal line, indicating bearish pressure on the stock. The combination of neutral RSI and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum, indicating that traders should be cautious in the current environment.

  • Key Resistance: $359.50
  • Key Support: $281.50

Analysts Think AVGO Has Room To Run

Investors are looking ahead to the next earnings report on Mar. 5.

  • EPS Estimate: $1.88 (Up from $1.60 YoY)
  • Revenue Estimate: $19.17 billion (Up from $14.92 billion YoY)
  • Valuation: P/E of 68.2x (Indicates premium valuation)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $450.96. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Wells Fargo: Upgraded to Overweight (Raises Target to $430.00) (Jan. 15)
  • RBC Capital: Initiated with Sector Perform (Target $370.00) (Jan. 15)
  • Mizuho: Outperform (Raises Target to $480.00) (Jan. 9)

Valuation Insight: While the stock trades at a premium P/E multiple, the strong consensus and 17% expected earnings growth suggest analysts view this growth as justification for the 41% upside to analyst targets.

Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for Broadcom, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Quality: Strong (Score: 96.76) — The balance sheet remains healthy.
  • Momentum: Bullish (Score: 77.86) — Stock is outperforming the broader market.
  • Value: Risk (Score: 7.18) — Trading at a steep premium relative to peers.

The Verdict: Broadcom’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a classic ‘High-Flyer’ setup. While the Momentum (77.86) confirms the strong trend, the low Value (7.18) score warns that the stock is priced for perfection—investors should ride the trend but use tight stop-losses.

Top ETF Exposure

  • Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (NYSE:VYM): 8.68% Weight
  • Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (NYSE:SPMO): 9.75% Weight
  • FT Cboe Vest Technology Dividend Target Income ETF (NASDAQ:TDVI): 9.62% Weight

Significance: Because AVGO carries such a heavy weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows for these ETFs will likely force automatic buying or selling of the stock.

AVGO Shares Drop Friday

AVGO Price Action: Broadcom shares were down 1.90% at $319.31 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Life360 Shares Climb After Boosting FY25 Sales Guidance
data.description:
Life360 Inc (NASDAQ:LIF) shares are popping on Friday after the company raised its fiscal year 2025 sales guidance.

Life360’s Bold Revenue Forecast Ignites Investor Interest

The company now expects FY2025 revenue of $486–$489 million, up from its prior range of $474–$485 million, which works out to roughly 31–32% year‑over‑year growth. Life360 also projects adjusted EBITDA of $87–$92 million, or an 18–19% margin.

The upbeat guidance follows a strong fourth-quarter. Global monthly active users climbed to 95.8 million, and the company added 16.2 million net new users in 2025, which is its best forth-quarter user growth ever.

Paying Circles also hit new highs: 2.8 million globally, with 576,000 net additions for the year, the largest annual increase on record. U.S. Paying Circles rose 23% to 2.0 million, while international subscribers grew 32% to 0.8 million.

Management said the updated outlook is based on preliminary, unaudited results and reflects broad strength across the business.

Are Bears Underestimating Life360’s Recovery Potential?

Life360 is currently trading in a challenging technical setup, with its price struggling below key moving averages. Specifically, Life360 stock is trading 16.9% below its 100-day SMA and 5.1% below its 200-day SMA, indicating bearish pressure in the longer-term trend.

The RSI is currently at 27.67, signaling that Life360 stock is in oversold territory. This suggests that the stock may be due for a bounce, but traders should be cautious as the momentum remains weak. MACD is below its signal line, indicating bearish pressure, suggesting that the stock may continue to face downward momentum unless a significant reversal occurs.

Key support is at $59.00, while resistance is at $80.50. If Life360 tests the support level, a break below could signal further declines, while a move above resistance might indicate a potential trend reversal.

In January, a death cross occurred when the 50-day SMA crossed below the 200-day SMA. This crossover typically signals a bearish trend, which traders should keep in mind as they assess the stock’s performance.

Looking at the 12-month performance, Life360 has gained 47.43%, suggesting that despite current challenges, there has been a longer-term upward trend. However, the recent price action indicates that traders should remain vigilant for signs of a trend reversal or continued weakness.

Currently positioned at 47.8% of its 52-week range, Life360 is closer to its recent lows than its highs. This positioning reflects the stock’s recent struggles and reinforces the need for traders to monitor key levels closely.

LIF Price Action: Life360 shares were up 24.72% at $68.36 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Miha Creative/Shutterstock.com

data.title: Trump Promised To Cut Your Energy Bill In Half — Why Are Costs Still Surging?
data.description:
Americans were promised cheaper energy. Instead, they're getting higher bills — just as a brutal winter tightens its grip.

Natural gas prices are surging at a historic pace, utilities are approving rate hikes, and households are bracing for higher heating and electric costs nationwide. The spike comes despite President Donald Trump‘s pledge on the campaign trail to cut U.S. energy prices in half — a reminder that energy markets often respond less to political promises than to weather, infrastructure limits and global supply dynamics.

  • State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSE:XLE) is climbing. See the chart and price action here. 

What’s At Stake?

Energy prices are rising, impacting most Americans but mostly low- and middle-income families, according to Mark Wolfe, executive director of the National Energy Assistance Directors Association (NEADA).

“These increases may be an inconvenience for higher-income households, but for low- and middle-income families they are devastating," Wolfe said in a Jan. 20 report. “Millions of households that were getting by are now being driven into utility debt and toward shutoffs because they cannot afford to keep their homes warm.”

Home heating costs are projected to rise 9.2% this season, the NEADA projects. That’s more than three times the rate of inflation, as higher electricity and natural gas prices collide with colder-than-average weather.

In Detroit, for example, wind chills are expected to drop to -25°F on Saturday, Jan. 24. High temperatures will stay far below normal, reaching only 9°F on Saturday, and 16°F on Sunday, compared with the January average of 32.3°F

The average U.S. household will spend roughly $995, up $84 from last year, with electric-heated homes seeing the steepest jump at 12.2%, and natural gas households up 8.4%. Heating oil and propane users are seeing smaller changes, as lower fuel prices offset the chill.

Is Extreme Cold Overwhelming The System?

Basically, yes. Short-term energy prices are largely governed by physics, weather and markets — not executive orders.

  • The U.S. is experiencing an exceptional cold wave across roughly 40 states, pushing heating demand sharply higher.
  • Natural gas fuels about 40% of U.S. electricity generation and heats more than half of American homes.
  • When temperatures plunge, gas demand spikes simultaneously for homes, power plants and industry.

That sudden, synchronized demand is why Henry Hub futures surged past $5 per MMBtu — an extraordinary weekly move.

The Trump administration’s anti-renewable policies can exacerbate this by forcing heavier reliance on volatile fossil fuel markets, rather than mitigating peak demand with solar, wind, or storage. Just yesterday, the Energy Department confirmed it was either revising or outright canceling more than $83 billion in loans for clean energy technologies.

Natural Gas Supply Can't Ramp Up Overnight

Despite record U.S. production, natural gas is not infinitely flexible.

  • Wells take time to drill and complete.
  • Pipeline capacity is fixed in the short term.
  • Storage withdrawals accelerate in extreme cold, tightening markets quickly.

Who Benefits From Higher Prices?

  • Cheniere Energy (NYSE:LNG)
  • TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE)
  • Freeport LNG (NASDAW: FLNG)
  • ExxonMobil (NYSE:XOM)
  • Chevron (NYSE:CVX)

Natural gas prices follow a boom-and-bust cycle typical of commodity markets. When prices rise, producers drill aggressively, boosting supply. Eventually, the market becomes oversupplied, prices fall, and unprofitable projects are shut down.

Over one to two years, reduced production combined with recovering demand pushes prices back up, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. Henry Hub natural gas is a clear example of this pendulum-like behavior.

Infrastructure Bottlenecks Amplify Price Spikes

In many regions, especially the Northeast:

  • Pipeline constraints limit how much gas can reach population centers.
  • Power grids rely on gas plants that compete with homes for fuel.
  • Cold weather increases the risk of equipment failures and outages.

This is why regions like New York and Connecticut often see disproportionately higher costs, even when national supply looks ample.

Utility Rate Hikes Reflect Past Costs

Take Con Edison’s increases approved in New York. In addition to this week's cold snap, factor in:

  • Rising labor and maintenance costs
  • Grid hardening and climate resilience investments
  • Inflation-driven equipment and financing expenses

In the meantime, weather and markets rule.

Global Forces Still Matter

U.S. energy markets are no longer insulated.

  • LNG exports link domestic gas prices to global demand.
  • Europe and Asia compete for supply during winter.
  • Any geopolitical disruption tightens markets further.

To sum up: The U.S. may be energy-rich, but it is not energy-isolated.

In 2024, data show Europe (including Türkiye) remained the top destination for U.S. LNG exports, accounting for 53% of total shipments. Exports to Asia grew from 26% in 2023 to 33% in 2024. Other regions — including the Middle East, North Africa, and Latin America — also saw rising imports, accounting for 14% of U.S. LNG exports, up from 8% in 2023.

Because U.S. LNG exports connect domestic natural gas prices to global markets, higher shipments can tighten local supply and push prices up. Export-related withdrawals can leave less gas for heating and power generation, further increasing energy bills.

Over the long term, higher prices can spur more production, but in the short term, exports can raise costs for U.S. consumers.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Meta Finds Its Next Monetization Lever
data.description:
Threads momentum, robots mature, crypto consolidates

META (NASDAQ:META) jumped roughly 5% as management highlighted early progress monetizing Threads. The market reaction was less about near-term revenue and more about optionality. Threads is being positioned as another scalable ad surface layered onto Meta's existing data and targeting engine. Importantly, this follows a familiar playbook: build engagement first, then monetize efficiently.

Elon Musk on Robots and the Long Game

(NASDAQ:TSLA) CEO Elon Musk reiterated that robotics remains a long-term strategic priority rather than an immediate revenue driver. The focus was on autonomy, learning systems, and real-world deployment, not aggressive timelines or commercialization promises. This tempers expectations while keeping the vision intact.

The key takeaway is that robots are an efficiency and productivity story before they are a product story. As with autonomy, progress will be uneven, but the advantage accrues to platforms that can integrate hardware, software, and data at scale.

Bitcoin Remains in a Downtrend

Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) continues to trade in a clear downtrend despite intermittent relief rallies. The discussion emphasized structure over sentiment, lower highs and weak follow-through still define price action. Risk appetite has not returned in a sustained way.

This does not invalidate the long-term thesis for crypto, but it does reinforce the need for patience. Until liquidity improves or macro pressure eases, Bitcoin is behaving like a risk asset still searching for a durable bottom.

Thanks for reading! For more updates throughout the week, follow @WOLF_Financial

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Elon Musk 'Wasn't Sure If Nobody Would Buy It'—But Had A Backup Plan To Ditch Cybertruck Design and Build One That 'Looks Like The Others'
data.description:
Elon Musk didn't design the Cybertruck for mass appeal. He designed it to intimidate.

The futuristic wedge on wheels was a gamble—and Musk knew it. "I wasn't sure if nobody would buy it or a lot of people would buy it," he said on the "Third Row Tesla" podcast in 2020.

Just in case it flopped, Musk had a Plan B. "I told the team, ‘Listen, if nobody wants to buy this, we can always make one that looks like the other trucks.'" He continued, "It was a weird failure? Fine. Just make one that looks like the others, and there you go."

Don't Miss:

  • Missed Nvidia and Tesla? RAD Intel Could Be the Next AI Powerhouse — Just $0.85 a Share
  • Sam Altman Says AI Will Transform the Economy — This Platform Lets Investors Back Private Tech Early

During the podcast, Musk opened up about everything from factory logistics to shipping nightmares through Tierra del Fuego. But it was his blunt admission about Cybertruck's risk—that it might totally tank—that stood out.

The Look That Turned Heads

The truck's design, he said, was inspired by "Blade Runner," "Mad Max," "Back to the Future," and "Aliens"—a literal "futuristic armored personnel carrier" meant to out-tough every other pickup on the market. Musk said that people in the U.S. buy pickup trucks because "it's like, which one is the most badass? Which one is the toughest? It's like, yeah, that one—like a tank."

He wasn't exaggerating. The stainless steel slab looked more like a tank from the future than something you'd find on a job site. But when Tesla unveiled it in late 2019, even some die-hard fans were confused. Musk himself was unsure whether anyone would actually buy it. 

His brother, Kimball, agreed it was a "daring" design choice—but said Elon was clearly the most excited about it. Elon doubled down, saying, "I think it's our best product ever."

Trending: It’s no wonder Jeff Bezos holds over $250 million in art — this alternative asset has outpaced the S&P 500 since 1995, delivering an average annual return of 11.4%. Here’s how everyday investors are getting started.

"Weird Failure" Is Starting to Look Familiar

Still, that “weird failure” Musk joked about hasn’t aged so well. 

Despite an initial surge of enthusiasm and an estimated 39,000 Cybertrucks sold in 2024, sales nearly halved in 2025. According to InsideEVs analysis of Cox Automotive data, only 20,200 were sold last year—a nearly 19,000-unit drop, the steepest decline for any EV model in the U.S., including some that were discontinued altogether.

A Truck Like No Other—Maybe Too Much So

The hype was real, though. 

Back in 2019, then-Kelley Blue Book executive publisher Karl Brauer called the performance and pricing specs "undeniable" and predicted that even if the look was polarizing, demand would still be high among Tesla loyalists. "How far beyond Tesla's customer base will the Cybertruck reach?" he asked. That's still an open question.

Musk, for his part, made it clear that part of the truck's purpose was to stand out. "Everything else is like the same," he said. "Variations on the same theme. You want to have something different."

And that, it definitely was. Even if sales suggest many buyers still prefer their trucks to look less like alien hardware and more like, well, trucks.

As Musk put it himself: "You don't just try it and then give up."

Musk takes wild swings—sometimes they land like rockets, sometimes like broken windows. But he's still the richest man in the world, and the Cybertruck won't be his last moonshot. Whether it redefines the pickup or fades into Tesla trivia, the bigger bet is always on what comes next.

For investors drawn to bold innovation—but without the risk of designing a stainless-steel tank—Fundrise offers a way to invest in private tech companies long before they go public. With just $10, you can back startups shaping the future, one smart bet at a time.

Image: Shutterstock
